<!-- loading: on-demand (invoked via the Skill tool when needed) — generated by scripts/list-skills.sh --annotate; do not hand-edit -->

# ast-grep structural rules (project template)

> **Project-scoped template.** Copy the rules you want into the project, adapt the
> language/pattern, then commit. ast-grep is an **optional adjunct** to Semgrep/OSV/Trivy,
> not a replacement — it shines at structural patterns that are awkward as regex.

## When to use it

Invoke `ast-grep` (CLI, `Bash(ast-grep:*)`) when a STRIDE efficacy question (U-02) or a
diff review needs a *syntax-shaped* check that a text regex would get wrong — e.g. "is a
CPU-hard call on the async event loop", "is this RLS policy created without FORCE". It
parses to an AST, so it does not false-match inside strings/comments the way `grep` does.

## Advisory-only contract (hard boundary)

ast-grep findings go into **`security-report.md` prose only**. They **must NOT** feed any
`security-status.json` count (`semgrep_findings`, `critical_count`, …) or any gate/loop-exit
conjunct. Reason: unlike the wrapped scanners (U-09 stamps + `reconcile-scans.sh`
recomputes their counts from hash-verified artifacts), ast-grep has **no wrapper and no
count reconciliation yet**. Until it gets one (TA A-4 backlog), an ast-grep hit is a
*lead to investigate*, never a number the gate trusts. If a hit reveals a real defect, the
fix flows through the normal exploitable-finding path and shows up in the reconciled tools'
counts on the next scan.

## Example rules (starter set — adapt per stack)

### R1 — sync KDF / blocking call on the async event loop (Python)
Pairs with the async-runtime efficacy question. Flags a bcrypt/argon2/scrypt call made
directly inside an `async def` (should be off-loaded to a thread pool).
```yaml
id: sync-kdf-in-async
language: python
rule:
  inside: { kind: function_definition, has: { field: async, regex: "async" } }
  pattern: $HASHER.$METHOD($$$)
constraints:
  HASHER: { regex: "bcrypt|argon2|scrypt|pbkdf2" }
note: CPU-hard KDF on the event loop blocks all concurrent requests — run it in a thread pool.
```

### R2 — RLS policy created without FORCE (SQL)
Pairs with the DB-privilege efficacy question. A table-owning role bypasses RLS unless the
table is `FORCE`d; a `CREATE POLICY` with no accompanying `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` is a
lead to check ownership.
```yaml
id: rls-without-force
language: sql
rule:
  pattern: CREATE POLICY $$$
note: If the app role owns this table, RLS is advisory without ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY — verify ownership.
```

## Running

```
ast-grep scan --rule <rule>.yml <path>      # one rule
ast-grep scan --config sgconfig.yml <path>  # a project rule set
```
Route any raw output you keep to `.pipeline/` (never the tree/scratchpad), same as the
other scanners. It does not need Docker — it is a single native binary.
